segments.s122-g81: برآمد is a past/stative verb 'arose/issued forth' placed at the clause start as is common in epic narration. ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' and هر دو سپه literally 'both hosts/armies' (هر دو = both). بوق و کوس are the items that 'arose' (trumpet and war-drum); the coordination with و simply lists the instruments.
segments.s122-g82: This is two short predications linked without repeating the copula: هوا نیلگون شد literally 'the air became indigo' (هوا subject, نیلگون predicate adjective, شد = became). زمین آبنوس uses the same verb implicitly: 'the earth (became) ebony' — Persian often drops the repeated verb and places the adjective after the noun.
segments.s122-g83: چو introduces a simile 'like/as.' برق درخشنده is a nominal phrase 'shining lightning' (برق noun, درخشنده adjective). از تیره میغ is an ablative phrase 'from the darkness/of the blades' where از marks origin and تیره ('dark') qualifies میغ ('blade'); word order places the adjective تیره before the head میغ in a poetic compactness.
segments.s122-g84: همی is an older narrative/adverbial particle here (marks ongoing or vivid action) attached to the past verb افروخت 'kindled/ignited' (past). آتش is the direct object ('fire' or 'flame') and از گرز و تیغ uses از to indicate source ('from maces and swords'); the clause describes sparks/fires springing from those weapons.
segments.s122-g85: هوا is the subject, گشت is the past tense 'became/turned,' and the coordinated adjectives سرخ و سیاه و بنفش are the result-state predicates ('red, black, and purple'). The verb precedes the adjectives (predicate after گشت) as a simple nominal predication.
segments.s122-g86: ز بس is a causal/ablative phrase 'from so many / because of the abundance of.' نیزه 'spears' and گونه‌گونه درفش 'various banners' are coordinated causes; گونه‌گونه is an adjectival/adverbial reduplication meaning 'of many kinds.' The whole phrase explains the reason for the air's colors.
segments.s122-g87: زمین شد 'the earth became' is followed by به کردار 'in the manner/likeness of' (به here introduces similarity), دریای قیر 'a sea of tar' is a simile: the noun دریای (genitive of دریا) modified by قیر. The overall structure is 'the earth became (as) a tar-sea.'
segments.s122-g88: همه موجش 'all its wave(s)' uses the possessive suffix -ش referring back to زمین; همه quantifies موجش. از introduces the material/source ('made of') and the list خنجر و گرز و تیر ('dagger, mace, and arrow') names the 'waves' — a continued metaphor: waves of the tar-sea are weapons.
segments.s122-g9: سراپرده شهریار و سران is a coordinated noun phrase: 'سراپردهِ شهریار' (the king's inner tent/pavilion) followed by 'و سران' (and the chiefs). In poetic Persian the ezāfe is often omitted, so 'سراپرده شهریار' reads as a genitive ('the king's inner pavilion'). Word order is noun + possessor (with no overt ezāfe) then 'and' + plural noun.
segments.s122-g10: کشیدند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb (they drew/moved). Here کشیدن + بر + place means 'to move toward/advance upon a place' (poetic usage). بر دشت مازندران is a prepositional phrase 'onto/over the plain of Mazandaran' where بر functions as 'on/onto' and مازندران is the place-name (mythic/epic region).
segments.s122-g11: This phrase strings nouns without an explicit ezāfe (common in epic verse): ابر میمنه طوس نوذر reads as 'the right-side cloud/banner of Tūs and Nowzar' or more loosely 'on the right (wing) were Tūs and Nowzar.' به پای is a locative prepositional unit 'at the foot (of)'; it prepares the next clause by locating the scene 'at the foot (of the mountain)'.
segments.s122-g12: دلِ کوه پر ناله کر نای is a verbless descriptive clause typical of Persian poetry: 'the heart of the mountain was full of the lament (ناله) of the nay (نای).' پر is used predicatively as 'full (of)'. کر here is an adjectival/intensifying word ('deaf' in literal sense) attached to نای to convey the force or loudness of the instrument (an archaic/poetic coloration).
segments.s122-g13: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. گودرز کشواد are two proper names placed together (no conjunction needed in verse). بر میسره is a prepositional phrase where بر = 'onto/toward' and میسره ('meisreh') is an adjective/nominal meaning 'attainable/reachable' or 'in easy reach' — it links to the following predicate (شده) so the full thought is completed in the next segment.
segments.s122-g14: شده is the past participle/perfect of شدن 'to become' ('had become'). کوه آهن is a noun phrase 'mountain of iron' (poetically: the mountain turned to iron/steel). زمین یکسره puts the ground after the noun as an adverbial phrase: زمین = ground/earth and یکسره = 'completely/utterly', giving the sense 'the earth was wholly so.' Word order and omission of auxiliary copula are normal in epic verse.
segments.s122-g15: سپهدار کاووس is a genitive construction (with the ezāfe omitted): 'the commander of Kavus' or 'Kavus's sepahdār.' در قلبگاه is a locative phrase meaning 'in the heart/center' (قلبگاه = core/center). Together they locate the commander at the center of the battle formation.
segments.s122-g16: ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز is the poetic form of از 'from'). رده برکشیده سپاه is an adjectival/participial construction: رده (ranks) + برکشیده (raised/drawn up, perfect/participle) qualifying سپاه (the army), so the phrase means 'the army, drawn up in ranks from every side.' The participle برکشیده modifies سپاه and yields the sense 'had been drawn up/arrayed.'
segments.s122-g65: پس = 'then' (narrative connective). پشت او is a genitive/possessive phrase 'his back' (پشت + enclitic pronoun او). اندر آمد is a poetic past construction 'came into / struck against' (اندر used with آمد). چو begins a simile 'as/when'. گرد here begins a compound with the following word (گردِ سِنان) meaning the round/part of the spear; word order puts this modifier before سنان.
segments.s122-g66: سنان is the spear (the agent/instrument in the image). بر marks contact 'on/against'; کمربند او is 'his belt' (کمربند + possessive او). راست here is an adverb/adjectival element 'straight/squarely', and کرد is the simple past auxiliary 'made/did' that completes the verbal phrase 'راست کرد' = 'struck straight'.
segments.s122-g67: بزد is the past tense of زدن 'he struck/struck at' placed before the object in this poetic narrative. نیزه is 'spear' (the object/instrument); بر + بند = 'upon the fastening/strap' (بر indicating contact). درع و زره are two words for types of armor ('cuirass and mail') listed in coordination.
segments.s122-g68: زره را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker emphasizing 'the mail'. نماند is the past of ماندن 'remained / was left' (here negative in sense with following ایچ). ایچ is an emphatic negator 'nothing / not at all'. بند و گره 'links and knots' names the fastenings that were left — the line says none remained.
segments.s122-g69: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. زینش = زین + -ش 'his saddle' (possessive enclitic). جدا کرد is a compound verb 'separated/detached' (جدا 'apart' + کرد 'did'). و برداشتش continues the action: برداشتش is برداشت + enclitic -ش 'he lifted/removed him', with برداشت as the past of برداشتن 'to take up / lift'.
segments.s122-g70: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'. بر here means 'to / onto' (direction toward someone). بابزن is a compound noun meaning a doorkeeper/gate‑attendant (one who strikes the door). مرغ 'bird' is the image of something small and easily carried. برگاشتش (برگرفت + enclitic) = 'he carried/took it up (to)', with the pronoun attached.
segments.s122-g71: بینداخت is the past of بینداختن 'he threw down / cast off', used here as the main verb. از پشت اسپش is a prepositional phrase 'from the back of his steed' (اسپش = اسپ + -ش 'his horse'). به خاک marks the destination 'onto the ground'.
segments.s122-g72: دهان پر ز خون is a verbless descriptive phrase 'his mouth full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'). و زره چاک چاک coordinates and adds 'and his armor châk châk' — the reduplicated adjective چاک چاک intensifies 'riven/gaping/torn'. The sequence is a standard Persian nominative description following the action.
segments.s122-g25: همی is a frequentative/continuative particle here (an ongoing or dramatic past sense). جوشن means 'cuirass/armor'; اندر + تنش is the locative phrase 'upon/over his body' where -ش is the enclitic possessive 'his'. برفروخت is a poetic past-tense verb with the prefix بر- + فروختن used here in the sense 'kindle/set afire' (archaic/poetic); the clause literally says he 'kindled the cuirass on his body.' Word order is Subject (understood) + object phrase + verb (typical older Persian permissive ordering).
segments.s122-g26: همی again gives a continual/dynamic sense. تف is an onomatopoeic interjection 'tuf' (a spit/scoff sound) used poetically to mark a sudden, contemptuous or violent motion. تیغش is 'his sword' (تیغ + possessive -ش). زمین را uses را as the definite direct-object marker ('the earth' as object). بسوخت is the simple past active 'burned/scorched' (here transitive: his sword scorched the earth). The clause is thus '(with a) tuf, his sword burned the earth.'
segments.s122-g27: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به ایران is the directional phrase 'into/to Iran'. سپه is an archaic/formal word for 'army, host'. برگذشت is a past verb with the prefix بر- meaning here 'passed by / passed through' (the host moved on). The sequence is verb phrases describing movement: 'the host came into Iran and passed on.'
segments.s122-g28: بتوفید is a poetic/archaic verb form built on توفیدن; in this context it functions figuratively of a violent outward movement or 'spouting/leaping forth' at a sound. از + آواز او ('from his voice') gives the cause/source: 'at his cry' or 'from his voice.' کوه و دشت is the coordinated subject 'mountain and plain' that respond to the verb. The whole clause means that at his cry mountain and plain leapt/moved away — a dramatic hyperbolic image.
segments.s122-g29: همی gives a continuous/dynamic coloring. گفت + باِ من here functions as 'he said to me' (گفت is the speech verb; the data's glosses list 'conversation' but syntactically this is the past-tense speech 'said'). که introduces reported content or a quotation/relative clause. جوید is present/subjunctival in form but here used in narrative sense 'seeks/wishes' (he who seeks). نبرد is the object 'battle/fight.' The clause frames reported speech: 'he said to me (that) someone seeks battle...' or as direct speech 'Who seeks battle?'
segments.s122-g30: کسی is 'someone/anyone.' کاو here is an archaic relative/pronoun form meaning 'who/that' (though its dictionary glosses list verbal senses, in classical poetry کاو functions as 'who'). برانگیزد is a present-subjunctive/optative-form verb meaning 'would rouse/should arouse' (used here as potential/ability: 'can rouse' or 'would rouse'). از آب گرد is a prepositional phrase 'from/away from around the water' (آب = water; گرد = 'around' in the locative sense). Together the clause asks whether there is anyone who can rouse him from about the water's edge.
segments.s122-g31: نشد is the negative past 'did not happen/was not possible' (here functioning as 'could not' or 'there was no one who...'). هیچکس is 'no one.' پیش جویان literally 'before the juyān'; in this epic context جویان means 'the seekers/pursuers' (not streams), so پیش جویان برون = 'go out before the pursuers' or 'advance ahead of the seekers.' برون is the adverb 'out/forth.' The clause states that no one did (or was able to) go out ahead of those seekers.
segments.s122-g32: This is a negative result clause introduced by نه ... نه 'neither ... nor.' رگشان 'their vein(s)' (رگ + possessive -شان). بجنبید is past-tense subjunctive/preterite 'stirred/moved' (here negative: did not move). در تن 'in their bodies' gives the location. نه خون 'nor blood' continues the negation—construed elliptically as 'nor did their blood (move or flow)'. The clause means literally 'not a vein of theirs moved in the body, nor (did) their blood.'
segments.s122-g1: چو is an archaic/poetic conjunction meaning “when” introducing a temporal clause. آگاهی (subject) + آمد (verb) is a normal noun‑verb order: “news arrived.” به + شخص (به کاووس شاه) is a dative/recipient phrase placed after the verb, literally “to King Kavus.”
segments.s122-g2: که introduces reported content (“that …”). تنگ اندر آمد is a compact poetic expression = “pressed/closed in” (تنگ = close, اندر = in, آمد = came). ز marks origin/agent (“from/of”), and سپاه is the head noun “army.” Note: دیوان here means the demonic beings (Divs) in the epic sense (this sense is not listed in the provided lemma senses).
segments.s122-g3: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb often used as “he commanded/said.” تا here introduces a subordinate purposive/jussive clause (“that/so that …”). رستم زال is the personal name (name + patronymic/epithet). زر immediately after the name functions as an epithet/attribute (“rich in gold” or “splendidly armed”), placed appositionally after the name in this poetic style.
segments.s122-g4: نخستین is an ordinal/adjective “first/foremost.” بران is an archaic adverbial/prepositional element (on/thereon/for him) linking the adjective to the following verb phrase. کینه is the object/qualifier (“hatred, vengeance”), and بندد is a 3rd‑person form used jussively/optatively here after تا (“should bind/gird”), literally “bind/gird.” کمر is the body part or girdle (“waist/girdle”) that is bound; together کینه بندد کمر = “gird his loins in/with hatred” (prepare for action in vengeance).
segments.s122-g5: Repeated به before proper names is the dative/recipient marker (“to/for Tūs and to Goudarz”). طوس here is the personal name Tūs (not the city). کشوادگان is an archaic collective/epithet attached after the names describing their function/status; the sequence places the dative phrase before the epithet in poetic order.
segments.s122-g6: Same pattern: به + proper names (to Giv, to Gorgin). آزادگان is an epithet following the names meaning “the free/noble men.” The construction is a list of recipients of the earlier command, with each name introduced by به.
segments.s122-g7: بفرمود تا again gives an order. لشکر is the subject “the army/host.” آراستند is a 3rd‑person plural past form (they adorned/arrayed themselves) used here after تا to report the commanded action — classical narrative often uses past forms for ordered actions.
segments.s122-g8: سنان و سپرها (spears and the shields) are placed before the verb in the typical poetic object‑before‑verb order. بپیراستند is a compounded verb (بـ + پیراستند) meaning “they equipped/arrayed/ornamented,” agreeing with the plural subject; together it says the spears and shields were made ready.
segments.s122-g73: This is a coordinated noun phrase: 'دلیران و گردان مازندران' = 'the brave ones and the regiments of Mazandaran.' In classical Persian the genitive relationship is shown by word order without an explicit ezāfe, so 'گردان مازندران' means 'the troops of Mazandaran.'
segments.s122-g74: 'به خیره فرو ماندند اندران' is a predication with a locative/complimentary cluster. 'به' here (= 'towards/into') introduces the state; 'خیره' = 'astonished/staring'; 'فرو' is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'deeply/completely'; 'ماندند' is the past plural of ماندن ('they remained') and is the main verb; 'اندران' is a classical locative adverb ('there/in that place') placed after the verb. The whole means they remained utterly astonished there.
segments.s122-g75: Basic S–V–Complement order in poetry: 'سپه' (the host) is the subject, 'شد' is the past copula 'became', followed by two adjectival descriptions. 'شکسته‌دل' is a compound adjective 'broken‑hearted', and 'زرد روی' ('زرد' + 'روی') functions as 'pale‑faced' (روی = 'face').
segments.s122-g76: 'برآمد' (arose) is a past action verb. 'ز' = 'from'. The phrase 'آورد گه' is a classical compression meaning 'the moment/occasion of the coming/attack' with 'آورد' used as a verbal noun ('the coming/bringing') and 'گه' = 'occasion/time'. 'گفت و گوی' is a binomial meaning 'conversation and speech/exchange of words'—so 'a talk/clamor arose from that moment.'
segments.s122-g77: 'بفرمود' is an honorific past verb here meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said.' Word order places the verb before the noun phrase 'سالار مازندران' (V–S), a common poetic order; 'سالار مازندران' = 'the commander of Mazandaran.'
segments.s122-g78: 'به یکسر سپاه' = 'to/unto the whole army' where 'به' is the preposition 'to,' 'یکسر' is an adverbial quantifier meaning 'entire/whole' modifying 'سپاه.' 'از کران تا کران' is a classical prepositional range 'from edge to edge' = 'from one end to the other' (with 'تا' = 'as far as/ to').
segments.s122-g79: 'که' introduces purpose/command here ('so that' / 'that'). 'یکسر' = 'all at once' or 'altogether.' 'بتازید' is the present-subjunctive/imperative second‑person plural of تاختن with the prefix بَـ used in classical Persian to form the jussive/imperative: 'you (pl.) charge/attack.' 'و' links the clauses and 'جنگ آورید' uses آورید (second‑person plural subjunctive/imperative) with 'جنگ' to mean 'engage in/wage war' (i.e., 'fight').
segments.s122-g80: 'همه' quantifies the following nouns: 'رسم و راه' ('manner and way' = customs and methods). 'پلنگ' is a genitive modifier ('of the panther') placed after the nouns in classical style (no ezāfe). 'آورید' is again second‑person plural subjunctive/imperative 'bring' or 'put into practice' — an order to adopt the leopard's manners and methods.
segments.s122-g17: A prepositional phrase in archaic/epic word order: به پیش سپاه = 'in front of / before the army' (به + پیش), followed by اندرون پیلتن = 'the interior (inside) of Piltan'. The whole phrase functions adverbially ('sent forward into the midst of the army, from the interior of Piltan').
segments.s122-g18: Relative clause introduced by که (that/who) modifying the person just sent forward. در جنگ sets the domain 'in battle'; هرگز ندیدی is the past negative 'you never saw'; شکن here is a substantive 'breaker' (i.e. one who is broken or who breaks) used idiomatically to mean 'one who is not seen broken / a breaker/defeater' — translated contextually as 'whom you had never seen broken/defeated in battle.'
segments.s122-g19: Simple noun phrase: یکی نامداری = 'a certain (one famed)', where یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' and نامداری (used as a substantive) indicates fame/notability; ز مازندران = 'from Mazandaran' (ز + place name).
segments.s122-g20: Verb phrase with a directional/locative complement: به گردن برآورده = literally 'raised to (his) neck' — برآورده is a past participle/compound used here like 'hoisted/put up' (archaic epic usage), with به indicating the target/location (to his neck/over his shoulder); گرز گران = 'a heavy mace' (گرز 'mace', گران 'heavy').
segments.s122-g21: Another relative/characterizing clause started by که 'who/that'. جویان (here) = 'seekers/pursuers' (not 'streams'); بدش نام = 'his name was terrible/evil to them' (literally 'to the seekers his name was bad') — a poetic way to say his reputation intimidated those who came after him; و جوینده بود = 'and he was a seeker' (repetition/emphasis on his active, searching/striking character).
segments.s122-g22: Predicate with two coordinated roles: گراینده گرز = 'one who directs/aims the mace' (گراینده = 'one who causes to turn/aim'), و گوینده بود = 'and was a speaker' (گوینده = 'speaker/orator'). The verb بود is the past copula 'was' attaching to both predicates.
segments.s122-g23: Instrumental/agentive prepositional phrase + verb: به دستوری = 'by an order' (به used instrumentally = 'by'), شاه دیوان = 'the king of the divs' (compound genitive), برفت is an archaic/past form meaning 'he went forth' (بر- prefixed past of رفتن, common in epic diction) — together: 'by the command of the king of the divs he went forth.'
segments.s122-g24: Motion toward a person: به پیش = 'to the presence of / before', سپهدار کاووس = 'the commander Kavus' (title + proper name), تفت is an archaic/epic verb form here meaning 'he presented himself / approached' (in context a verb of approaching or appearing before someone). The phrase as a whole means 'he went and stood before Commander Kavus.'
segments.s122-g49: به آورَد گه رفت is an adverbial-time phrase plus past verb: به + آورد + گه together mean “at the moment/occasion that (fortune) brought”; رفت is 3rd person past “he went/moved.” چون پیل مست is a simile placed after the verb: چون “like/as,” پیل “elephant,” مست “drunk,” i.e. “he moved like a drunken elephant.”
segments.s122-g50: یکی پیل is an indefinite noun phrase “one/ a certain elephant” (یکی before the noun for ‘one/a certain’). زیر اژدهایی به دست is a short prepositional/instrumental cluster: زیر “under/beneath,” اژدهایی “a dragon” (indefinite), به دست literally “by/at the hand (of)” — the copula is omitted (poetic), so the sense is ‘one elephant was under a dragon’s hand’ (i.e. in the dragon’s grip).
segments.s122-g51: عنان را بپیچید places the object (عنان ‘the reins’) before the verb with the object marker را; بپیچید is 3rd person past (he twisted/pulled). و برخاست links another past action ‘and he rose.’ گرد follows برخاست as a post-verbal noun meaning ‘dust’ (poetic inversion — in prose one might expect گرد برخاست).
segments.s122-g52: ز بانگش is an ablative/instrumental phrase: ز (poetic ‘from/because of’) + بانگش ‘his cry’ (بانگ + enclitic ش = his). بلرزید is 3rd person past ‘trembled.’ دشت نبرد is a compound noun ‘battle-plain’ (the object/subject that trembled). Word order is compact and elliptic — the prepositional phrase comes before the verb as typical in poetry.
segments.s122-g53: به جویان is an indirect-object phrase ‘to the juyān’ (here ‘to the onlookers/seekers’). چنین گفت is a fixed idiom ‘said thus/so’ (چنین ‘thus,’ گفت ‘said’). کای is the archaic vocative particle “O” addressing someone or something; بد نشان is a noun phrase ‘bad/ill sign’ (adjective before noun).
segments.s122-g54: بیفگنده is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning ‘unthrown/untainted/intact’ modifying نامت ‘your name’ (نام + enclitic ت = your name/reputation). ز introduces the agent/source ‘by/from,’ and گردنکشان is the agent noun ‘the neck-breakers/rebels’ (plural). The copula is omitted because the participle and noun express a verbless clause: ‘your name remains unspoiled by the rebels.’
segments.s122-g55: کنون ‘now’ is an adverb. بر تو is a dative/benefactive ‘to you/for you’; بر جای is an idiomatic collocation here (‘there is place/room’ — بر + جای). بخشایش is the noun ‘forgiveness/mercy’ and است is the copula ‘is.’ The phrase therefore means ‘now there is room/occasion for mercy toward you.’
segments.s122-g56: نه negates the following idea; هنگام آوُرد (هنگام + آورد) is a nominal/time expression ‘the time for bringing/setting forth’ (آورد here in a nominalized/lexical sense ‘bringing/attack’), و links the next noun آرامش ‘tranquility/repose.’ است is the copula. The construction says ‘it is not the time for (such) bringing/assault, nor (is it) a time of rest,’ i.e. negating both aggressive action and tranquil respite in this context.
segments.s122-g121: This is a range expression headed by the poetic preposition ازان (’from that’). ازان ... تا introduces the spatial extent: ازان میمنه (’from that right flank’) تا بدان (’to that [point]’). هنا بدَان is the combination of the preposition بِـ (here written ب) + دان (here functioning as the demonstrative ’that’), and میسره is a predicative adjective/noun meaning ‘within reach, attainable’ that completes the phrase (‘as far as that point that was within reach’). Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase + تا + target; the copular/qualifying notion is carried by میسره rather than an explicit verb.
segments.s122-g122: بشد is a past/formal perfective (literally ‘it became/happened’) introducing the action of the subject that follows; the sequence بشد گیو = ‘Giv became / Giv was’ (i.e. ‘Giv moved/acted’). چون introduces a simile (‘like’), followed by the simile’s image گرگ (wolf) and the target of attack پیش بره (’before/against a lamb’), where پیش functions adverbially/locatively ('in front of / upon') and بره is the object. Word order places the verb-like بشد before the subject (inversion often found in epic style) and the simile after the subject.
segments.s122-g123: ز (poetic form of از) marks the starting point ‘from’. شبگیر here names the onset/visitor of night (‘nightfall’), and تا introduces the temporal limit ‘until’. تیره شد آفتاب literally ‘the sun became dark’ is a passive/experiential clause: آمد/شد with آفتاب as subject → ‘the sun grew dark’ (شد = become/happen). The whole segment gives a time span: ‘from nightfall until the sun became dark.’
segments.s122-g124: همی is a frequent narrative particle in epic Persian indicating continuous or habitual action (‘kept / continuously’). خون به جوی اندر آمد is a motion clause: خون (subject) + به جوی اندر آمد (‘came into the stream’), where به + جوی marks direction/goal and اندر = ‘into/within’. چو آب is a simile ‘like water’. Word order is subject + directional phrase + verb with a trailing simile, and همی makes the verb آمد iterative/continuous.
segments.s122-g125: ز marks source/origin here: ‘from the face’ (ز چهره). بشد is the past/becoming verb (‘became / was made’), taking as its complement the coordinated nouns شرم و آیین مهر (‘shame and the custom/rites of love/affection’), meaning they departed/ceased on his face. The coordination with و links the two nouns as what was lost from the face; the clause is compact, with an implied verbal sense ‘were gone/left’.
segments.s122-g126: همی marks continuous action again (‘kept’). گرز بارید is a short verbal clause where گرز (mace) is the subject and بارید (rained/fell) is the verb: ‘maces rained down’. گفتی سپهر is a parenthetical phrase; گفتی here is used in an archaic/poetic sense close to ‘one might say / it is said’ (the provided lexical entry did not include this idiomatic sense, so it is treated as an idiomatic/poetic usage), and سپهر names the source ‘the sky/heavens’ — together ‘one might say, from the sky.’ The order places the continuous marker and subject-verb clause before the parenthetical speaker/source.
segments.s122-g127: ز introduces the source/agent ‘from the slain’ (ز کشته). به هر جای is a locative phrase ‘in/at every place’ (به + هر جای). بر توده گشت is a stative/result clause: بر توده = ‘into/onto a heap’ (بر as locative/onto) and گشت = ‘it turned / it became / it was formed’ (perfective), so the clause means that heaps (of bodies) were formed everywhere. Word order follows classical Persian: source + locative phrase + resultative verb.
segments.s122-g128: گیاها (plants/vegetation) is the subject; به مغز سر is a complex phrase meaning ‘to/at the core (mâġz) of the head’ — مغز here = ‘core’ or ‘brain’ and سر = ‘head’, the phrase describing the part of the plant or the part soiled; آلوده گشت is the compound predicate ‘became soiled/tainted’ (گشت = became). The preposition به marks the affected part, and the verb گشت makes the state change explicit. The overall sense: the vegetation was fouled even to its core/head.
segments.s122-g105: بپوشید is a third-person past tense verb ('he put on/covered'); the prefixed ب- is the classical narrative form. ازان پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that / from that time'. به + مغفر سرش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'onto his head the maghfar' (مغفر = a head-covering), with ش as the enclitic possessive 'his'. The whole segment reads literally 'he put on, after that, the maghfar on his head.'
segments.s122-g106: بیامد is a simple past tense verb 'came' (narrative past). بر is the preposition 'on/upon/toward' and links to لشکرش as its object/target. نامور modifies لشکرش: 'his renowned army'. The clause means 'his renowned army came (forward/onto the scene).'
segments.s122-g107: خروش آمد is a common narrative formula: خروش 'a cry/roar' + آمد 'came/rose' = 'a roar arose'. و links the next nominal phrase. ناله کرنای is a genitive construction (نالهٔ کرناى) meaning 'the lament/cry of the trumpet' (classical poetry frequently omits the ezafe particle). Here ناله functions as the object/phenomenon accompanying the roar.
segments.s122-g108: بجنبید is a past-plural/narrative form 'they moved/stirred' (used for the army). چون introduces a simile 'like/as'. کوه modifies لشکر in the image 'like a mountain, the army' and ز جای is a compound adverbial 'from its place' (ز = 'from' (poetic), جای = 'place'). Word order is figurative: 'they moved, like a mountain, the army, from its place.'
segments.s122-g109: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a high commander). بفرمود is an honorific past tense verb 'he commanded/ordered' (ب‑ prefixed polite/narrative past). تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result 'that/so that', followed by the names گیو و طوس as the clause's subjects/agents.
segments.s122-g110: به پشت سپاه is the goal phrase 'to the rear of the army' (به = 'to/for', پشت = 'rear/behind', سپاه = 'army'). اندر آرند is a classical-subordinate structure: اندر = 'into/within' and آرند is a form of آوردن used as a subjunctive/optative 'bring (them)' introduced by تا — together 'that they should bring/put in'. کوس is the object 'war-drum'. The literal reading: 'that Giv and Tous should bring the war-drums into the rear of the army.'
segments.s122-g111: چو is a comparative/simile particle 'like/as'. گودرز is the proper name 'Goudarz'. با is the preposition 'with'. زنگه here functions as a proper name (Zangah) rather than the common noun, and شاوران is plural 'the counselors/advisers'. The segment lists one grouping: 'as Goudarz with Zangah and the advisers.'
segments.s122-g112: Another parallel introduced by چو 'as/like': رهام (Roham) و 'and' گرگین (here the proper name Gorgin rather than adjective) form the named pair, followed by جنگ‌آوران 'the warriors' which apposites or describes them. The structure is a poetic cataloging: 'as Roham and Gorgin, the warriors.'
segments.s122-g57: بگرید is a jussive/optative form (classical Persian) meaning 'let him/her/they weep' (third‑person wish/imperative). ترا is the postposed object 'for you' (ت = 2nd‑person suffix attached to the object). آنک introduces a relative clause 'who/that', and زاینده بود is a past copular clause: زاینده 'giver/begetter' (noun/participle) + بود 'was' (past copula).
segments.s122-g58: This short clause offers alternatives. فزاینده بود is another simple past clause ('was an augmenter / was increasing'); ار is a classical conditional/alternative particle ('if' / 'whether'), introducing the alternative گزاینده بود ('was a chooser'). The repeated بود marks each alternative as a past copula.
segments.s122-g59: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (classical short form of به + او). گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (classical narrative usage). جویان is the subject 'the seekers/people searching'; که introduces the quoted injunction 'that / (i.e.)'. ایمن مشو is an imperative/negative command: ایمن 'safe' + مشو 'do not become' = 'do not be safe/secure'.
segments.s122-g60: This continues the warning with two prepositional phrases. ز جویان = 'from (the) seekers' (ز = poetic 'from'); و = 'and' links the clauses; از خنجر سرد رو is a prepositional phrase with از 'from/of', خنجر 'dagger', سرد 'cold' used adjectivally, and رو as noun 'face/front' (i.e. 'the cold face/point of the dagger') — together 'and turn away from the cold dagger' (verb 'turn' is implied by the imperative earlier).
segments.s122-g61: که here introduces the explanatory clause 'that/because'. اکنون = 'now'; به introduces the experienced state 'to/for' (here 'for the pain'); درد جگر مادرت is a genitive chain: درد 'pain/sorrow' + جگر used metaphorically as 'heart/seat of feeling' + مادر + ت (your mother). The clitic ت marks possession 'your'.
segments.s122-g62: بگرید repeats the jussive/imperative 'let him/her/they weep'. بدین is a prepositional expression (ب = to/for + دین here used idiomatically as 'this/manner' in older usage) meaning 'for/with this'; جوشن is 'cuirass/armor' (object), و connects, and مغفرت is 'forgiveness/mercy' (also object). Grammatically, the phrase tells the addressee to weep in relation to these (armor and forgiveness).
segments.s122-g63: چو functions temporally 'when'. آواز جویان is a noun + genitive: 'the voice/sound of the seekers'. به رستم رسید is the verb phrase 'reached Rostam' (به = to/toward, رستم = proper name, رسید = past 'reached/arrived').
segments.s122-g64: خروشی is a noun 'a roar/clamor'. چو again is a simile 'like/as'. شیر ژیان is a compound image 'lion (شیر) of life/ferocity (ژیان)' — here simply 'a lion' or 'a fierce lion'. برکشید is a past tense transitive verb 'he raised/he let forth' (here 'let out a roar').
segments.s122-g97: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of' (به + پیش). جهاندار is the noun 'sovereign' that follows and is the head of the phrase. گیهان خدای is a vocative/title sequence ('world' + 'O God' or 'God of the world') addressed to the supreme ruler; word order (modifier before noun) is typical of classical Persian epithets.
segments.s122-g98: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. همی بود is a classical/perfective combination where همی (progressive/habitual marker) with بود (past of 'to be') yields a continuous or sustained past state ('was continually/kept on being'). گریان is a predicative adjective 'weeping' and به پای is a prepositional phrase 'at (his) feet' (پای = foot/base). Together: 'he came and remained weeping at his feet.'
segments.s122-g99: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that / from then on'. بمالید is past tense 'he rubbed' (classical narrative past). بر خاک specifies the surface 'on the earth/dust' and روی (face) is the direct object placed after the prepositional phrase, giving the sense 'he rubbed his face on the ground' (a gesture of prostration).
segments.s122-g100: چنین گفت is a formulaic reporter 'thus said'. گفت here functions as the past narration 'he said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' (classical), followed by titles/epithet داور راستگوی ('judge, truthful one') addressed to the addressee; the sequence is direct speech introduced by the formulaic preceding clause.
segments.s122-g101: برین is an archaic/poetic concatenation of بر + این ('upon/over these'). نره modifies دیوان (here 'نره دیوان' = fierce/robust divs/demons) with بی‌بیم و باک as a coordinating adjective phrase 'fearless and without dread'. Grammatically the phrase sets up a contrast/condition before the copular assertion in the next segment. Note: دیوان as 'demons' is the intended Shahnameh sense (not present in the supplied senses list).
segments.s122-g102: تویی is the emphatic copula 'thou art / you are' (second-person present). آفریننده is the predicate noun 'creator' and آب و خاک is a coordinated noun phrase 'water and earth' that specifies what is created. Word order is subject (implicit you) + predicate: 'You are the creator of water and earth.'
segments.s122-g103: مرا is the accusative/object 'me' (here split in the word list into م + را; grammatically it functions as the direct object placed before the imperative, a common classical Persian order). ده is the imperative 'give'. تو is a vocative/emphatic 'you' addressing the addressee. پیروزی و فرهی are the coordinated direct objects 'victory and nobility/renown' being requested.
segments.s122-g104: به من is the indirect object 'to/for me'. تازه کن is an imperative compound: تازه (fresh/new) + کن (do) = 'renew / make fresh'. تخت شاهنشهی is the direct object 'the imperial throne' (تخت = throne; شاهنشهی = imperial/royal rule). The verb تازه کن governs the following object, producing 'renew the imperial throne for me.'
segments.s122-g89: دوان is an attributive active-participle-like adjective placed before باد to describe it ('the rushing wind'). پایان here functions adverbially ('at last' / 'in the end') modifying the clause, and چو + کشتی بر آب is a simile ('like a ship on the water') with بر governing آب.
segments.s122-g90: سوی + غرق together form a directional phrase 'toward sinking'; دارند is the present auxiliary-like form meaning 'they are (doing)' or 'they head' (here marking ongoing/middle voice motion). گویی is a parenthetical 'as if / it seems' (not listed in the provided senses). شتاب is a noun 'haste' that completes the simile ('as if with haste').
segments.s122-g91: همی is the iterative/continuous marker meaning 'kept on' or 'continually'; گرز is the subject noun 'maces'; بارید is simple past 'fell/rained' used metaphorically ('rained down'); بر خود is a prepositional phrase 'upon themselves' and ترگ is a noun 'rag/shred' standing with و to indicate also their torn clothing.
segments.s122-g92: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'; باد خزان is the compound 'autumn wind' (wind of autumn); بارد here functions verbally ('blows/causes to fall') though the supplied list lacks that sense; از بید برگ is a genitive phrase 'leaves from the willow' completing the image.
segments.s122-g93: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase 'within/after one week' (به marking the time). دو لشکر is the noun phrase 'two armies/hosts'; نامجوی is an adjective or epithet 'name-seeking / fame-seeking' describing those hosts.
segments.s122-g94: به روی forms part of the idiom 'to the face of / face-to-face'; اندر آورده بودند is a compound past construction 'had brought in/put' (آورده in past with بودند as past auxiliary), so the whole line means '[they] had brought (them) face to face'. The final روی repeats 'face' as the object in the idiom.
segments.s122-g95: به هشتم is a temporal expression 'on the eighth [day]'. جهاندار is an honorific 'sovereign' used appositionally; کاووس شاه is the name + title 'Kavus the king' (apposition: 'the sovereign, King Kavus').
segments.s122-g96: ز سر برگرفت is a verbal phrase 'took off / took from the head' (ز سر = 'from the head', برگرفت = past 'removed/took up'); آن کیانی کلاه is the direct object 'that Kayanian cap' (آن = 'that', کیانی = 'Kayanian/royal', کلاه = 'cap/crown').
segments.s122-g41: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). دستور باشد is an optative/subjunctive phrase meaning 'let there be an order' (باشد is a subjunctive/irrealis form of بودن). مرا را شهریار is the object phrase 'me (مرا + object marker را) as king' — the object pronoun مرا (split here into م + را) is the patient of the implied action 'make', and شهریار is the noun complement 'king'. The clause thus says 'that there be an order that I be made king.'
segments.s122-g42: شدن is the infinitive 'to become / to be made' functioning as the verbal complement to شهریار (i.e. 'to be made king'). پیش here means 'in the presence of / facing', and این دیو ناسازگار is the noun phrase 'this hostile / uncooperative demon' with ناسازگار as a postpositive adjective. Together: 'to be made king to face this ill‑disposed demon.'
segments.s122-g43: بدو (bedo) is a prepositional phrase meaning “to him/unto him” and comes before the verb for emphasis; گفت (goft) is the past, 3rd-person singular of “say” (here rendered idiomatically as “said”); کاووس (Kavus) is the subject placed after the verb (a common classical order: object/indirect object – verb – subject). کاین is a contracted classical form of که این (“that this”), introducing the content clause, کار is “matter/task/deed,” and تست is the archaic contracted copula توست (“is yours”) written as one word in poetic language.
segments.s122-g44: از ایران is a prepositional phrase “from Iran” (here indicating origin or source). نخواهد is the classical negative-future auxiliary (“will not” / here functioning as ‘will’ in a negative construction) and کس following it yields the sense “no one” (نخواهد کس = ‘no one will’). این جنگ is “this war/this fight,” and جست is an archaic poetic verb meaning roughly “to take up/enter/engage in” (literally ‘leapt into’ in some uses), so the clause says that no one from Iran will take up this battle. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis: prepositional phrase – auxiliary – indefinite – object – main verb.
segments.s122-g45: چو is the subordinating particle 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. ازو 'from him' marks the source of the speech, and این سخن is the object 'these words / this speech'. پهلوان (the champion) appears after the object by poetic inversion and is the agent who heard.
segments.s122-g46: بیامد is the past 'he came' or 'he advanced'. به کردار is a fixed phrase 'in the manner/character of', کردار meaning 'conduct, behavior'. شیر ژیان is a lion‑like expression: شیر 'lion' modified by ژیان (here conveying the manner/way of life), so the phrase means 'he came on in the manner of a lion' (i.e. boldly/ferociously).
segments.s122-g47: برانگیخت is the past tense 'he roused' or 'he set in motion'. رخش is the direct object 'his steed' and دلاور (the brave one) functions as the agent/subject placed after the object by poetic inversion. ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from its place' (ز = from, جای = place).
segments.s122-g48: به چنگ is an instrumental phrase 'with/by (his) grip / in his hand' (به + noun of grasp). اندرون here means 'inward/inside' and modifies the following phrase. نیزه سر گرای is a compact phrase: نیزه 'spear', سر 'head/point', and گرای (an adjectival/‑oriented element) meaning '‑facing/‑directed'; together implying 'with the spear's head directed inward'. Grammatically, the line strings instruments and direction together: 'with a grip, inward, the spear's head directed (so).'
segments.s122-g193: "چنین گفت" is a short reporting clause meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = thus, گفت = said). "کاوردم" is an archaic poetic past first‑person singular ('I struck/hew/probed'), and its object follows: "ان لخت کوه" = 'that bare piece/slab of mountain' (ان = that; لخت = bare; کوه = mountain). The word order is literary: reporting clause + direct speech verb + object phrase.
segments.s122-g194: This is a short clause with cause and result: "ز" = 'from/because of', "بیم" = 'fear'. "تبر شد" uses شد (become) with تبر (axe) as the grammatical subject: 'the axe became...'. "به چنگم" is a locative/possessive phrase 'in my grip / in my hand' (به + چنگ + -م). "ستوه" is a predicate adjective 'weary/tormented'. The whole means 'from fear the axe became weary in my hand.'
segments.s122-g195: "برویش" is a prepositional/directional phrase here ('toward him' / 'at him') in a compact poetic form. "نگه کرد" is the compound verbal expression 'cast a look / looked' (نگه = gaze, کرد = did/made). The subject "کاووس شاه" follows the verb for literary emphasis: 'Kavus the king looked at him.'
segments.s122-g196: "ندیدش" is negative past of دیدن with the object suffix -ش ('he did not see/regard him'); in this context it functions idiomatically as 'he did not consider him'. "سزاوار" is a predicate adjective 'worthy/deserving of'. "تخت و کلاه" are the objects: 'throne and (royal) cap/crown.' The clause means 'he did not regard him as worthy of the throne and crown.'
segments.s122-g197: "وزان" = وَ + زان (and from/and of that) linking to what precedes. "رنجهای کهن" is a genitive phrase 'the old/ancient sufferings' (رنجهای = sufferings, کهن = ancient). "یاد کرد" is the verbal phrase 'he remembered' (یاد + کرد = remember/made memory). The whole clause: 'and from those ancient sufferings he remembered' (i.e., he recalled them).
segments.s122-g198: "دلش خسته شد" is a simple result clause: دلش ('his heart') + خسته شد ('became tired'). "سر پر از باد کرد" is an idiomatic result/causative turn: سر ('head') + پر از باد ('full of wind') + کرد ('he made / it became') — literally 'he made his head full of wind.' In Persian poetry 'head full of wind' conveys emptiness, vanity, or being distracted; grammatically کرد is the verb applying to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s122-g199: "به دژخیم فرمود" = 'he commanded/said to the torturer' (به marks the indirect object/recipient, دژخیم = torturer, فرمود = he commanded/said). "تا" introduces purpose/result here ('so that / in order that'). "تیغ تیز" = 'a sharp blade' (تیغ = blade/sword, تیز = sharp), the object of the intended action.
segments.s122-g200: "بگیرد کند" contains two verbs forming the purposive/subjunctive sequence introduced by تا: بگیرد (subjunctive/present of گرفتن, 'to take/seize') and کند (subjunctive/present of کردن, 'to do/make'); together they express the commanded actions ('take and do ...'). "تنش را" marks the definite object 'his body' (تن + -ش = his, را = object marker). "ریز ریز" is a reduplication for emphasis meaning 'into tiny bits / bit by bit.' Grammatically, را attaches to تنش as the object of کند (cut/make).
segments.s122-g177: گو here functions as a short discourse particle meaning 'so/then' (a poetic/archaic linkage). پیلتن is a proper name; کرد is the light past verb 'did/made' used with the following nominal phrase چنگال باز (lit. 'claws open') to mean 'opened/spread his claws.' Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: particle + name + light verb + noun phrase.
segments.s122-g178: بران here means 'thereupon/for that' introducing the result; آزمایش is 'test/trial'; نبودش is the past negation 'it was not' (نبود + enclitic -ش 'it'); نیاز is 'need.' The phrase literally reads 'for that a test was not needed' — a causal/result clause following the previous action.
segments.s122-g179: بران گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner/so' (بران = 'thereupon/so', گونه = 'manner, way'). آن سنگ را is a definite direct object (آن 'that' + سنگ 'stone' + را object marker). برگرفت is the past tense 'he took up/raised' and comes at the end as the finite verb, following classical Persian word order.
segments.s122-g180: کزو is 'from which/therefrom' indicating cause/result; ماند is the past 'remained/was left' (passive/resultive sense). لشکر سراسر شگفت is the subject phrase 'the army, entirely astonished' where سراسر 'entire' intensifies and شگفت 'wonder/astonishment' describes their state. The clause means 'from which the whole army remained in wonder.'
segments.s122-g181: ابر is used metaphorically ('like a cloud' or 'in a cloud-like mass'), introducing how the people acted together; کردگار is 'the Creator/God'; آفرین is an acclamation 'praise/bravo'; خواندند is the past plural 'they called/cried' (here 'they cried out the acclamation'). Together: '(Like a cloud) they cried "Praise the Creator."'
segments.s122-g182: برو is here a brief prepositional/adverbial element meaning 'thereupon/on that' (poetic contraction of بر او / 'upon him' or 'thereon'); زر و گوهر are the items 'gold and gems' with و linking them; برافشاندند is the past plural 'they showered/scattered' (they cast wealth upon him). The clause describes the people showering treasures after their acclamation.
segments.s122-g183: به پیش سراپرده شاه is a prepositional phrase 'into the presence of the king's inner chamber' (به + پیش = 'to before/in the presence of'; سراپرده = 'inner tent/inner chamber'; شاه = 'the king'). برد here is a past tense of بردن 'to bring/take' (poetic past), meaning 'they brought (him).' Note: برد is used as a finite past verb though one of the glossed senses given is not the verbal meaning; context shows it is the verb 'brought.'
segments.s122-g184: بیفگند is a past verb 'they cast/they threw (him down)' (classical/poetic past form), followed by و 'and' linking the next action. ایرانیان را uses را as object marker on 'the Iranians,' and سپرد is the past 'they entrusted/handed over.' The sequence lists two actions: they cast him down and then delivered him to the Iranians.
segments.s122-g33: به آواز is an adverbial phrase meaning "in a voice / aloud" (به + noun used adverbially). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' and appears before its subject (شهریار) because poetic word order often places the verb early; آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' modifying the speech; شهریار is the subject 'the king'.
segments.s122-g34: به marks the indirect object 'to' — "to the ..."; گردان هشیار is a noun + adjective sequence ('troop/company' + 'alert'), and مردان کار is another noun phrase 'men of deeds' (men experienced in action). و simply links the two groups as recipients of the king's speech.
segments.s122-g35: که introduces the following explanatory/subordinate clause ('and/that'); زین here is the archaic contraction meaning 'from these' (the listed lemma senses do not match this use, so marked below); دیوتان is the plural 'these demons/ogres'; سر is the grammatical subject ('their heads'); خیره شد is a compound verbal phrase 'became fixed/stared' (خیره as adjective/participial notion + شد = became). چرا is an interrogative/exclamatory adverb ('how/why') used here in an exclamatory sense.
segments.s122-g36: از آواز او is a prepositional phrase 'from/at his voice' giving the cause; رویتان is the noun 'faces' with an older/plural/pronominal ending (here meaning 'their faces') and is the subject of تیره شد ('became dark'). Word order puts the cause phrase first, then subject, then predicate (tireh shod).
segments.s122-g37: ندادند is the negative past tense 'they did not give' (ن- prefix + دادند); پاسخ is the direct object 'reply/answer'; دلیران is the plural subject 'the brave ones'; به شاه is the indirect object 'to the king'. The verb appears before its subject here (poetic inversion).
segments.s122-g38: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; جویان literally 'streams' (used here metaphorically); بپژمرد is an archaic/perfective-form verb meaning 'withered/decayed' (ب‑ prefix with past sense); گفتی is ambiguous in this line (the listed senses are 'conversation' and do not fit cleanly — it functions here as a poetic particle or modifier, so marked below as uncertain); سپاه is 'the host/army'. The clause reads as a metaphorical description: the army seemed to lapse or wilt away.
segments.s122-g39: یکی is an indefinite 'one / suddenly / someone' used here to introduce a single action; برگرایید is an archaic/past form 'turned about' or 'turned toward'; رستم is the proper name 'Rostam' (the agent) and عنان 'the reins' is the object. Poetic order places the verb near the front and the actor/name after it.
segments.s122-g40: بر is the preposition 'toward/against'; شاه is the goal 'the king'; شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/came to be'; تاب‌داده is a participial descriptor 'having been swung' or 'brandished' modifying سنان; سنان here is the weapon 'the spear' (the swung spear is thus said to be directed at the king). The participle follows the auxiliary and the noun it modifies appears immediately after it in poetic order.
segments.s122-g129: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. رعد خروشنده شد is a compact predicate: رعد (subject) + خروشنده (present-participle/adjective 'roaring') + شد (past of شدن 'became'), literally 'the thunder became roaring' = 'the thunder roared'. The nominative phrase بوق و کوس follows as an asyndetic continuation: it names the trumpets and drums that then sounded (the verb for their sounding is implied rather than repeated).
segments.s122-g130: خور is the noun 'sun' and stands before a locative phrase; اندر پس پرده آبنوس is a compact locative: اندر = 'in/within', پس here functions as 'behind/after' (poetic use) and پرده آبنوس = 'ebony curtain/veil' (نإون + adjective). The copular verb ('was'/'lay') is omitted and understood: 'the sun (was) behind an ebony veil.'
segments.s122-g131: ازان سو 'from that side' (ازان = 'from that', سو = 'side'). The relative particle که introduces a clause qualifying that side: که بد شاه مازندران = 'where the evil king of Mazandaran (was)'. بد is an adjective 'bad/evil' modifying شاه; the verb 'was' is understood from the following clause.
segments.s122-g132: بشد is the past/perfective of شدن used here with the prior subject (the evil king) and means 'came to be/arrived' or 'appeared' at the place named next: پیلتن (a place-name). با سپاهی گران is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with a heavy/formidable army' (با = 'with', سپاهی = 'army/soldiers', گران = 'heavy/weighty' used figuratively = formidable). Word order reflects classical Persian: verb (بشد) links back to the subject in the previous segment.
segments.s122-g133: زمانی نکرد is a negated past expression 'did not (for a time)' or 'for a while did not...'; نکرد is the past negative of کردن 'to do' (here idiomatically 'to leave/yield'). او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. یله جای خویش is an idiomatic phrase 'leave his place/position' (یله here conveys the idea of 'leaving/vacating' or 'loosing hold of' in this context; جای خویش = 'his place/post').
segments.s122-g134: بیفشارد is a past-form verb meaning 'he pressed/crushed/forced down' (فشرد with an older prefixal form). It takes the locative بر کینه گه 'upon the spot/seat of hatred' (بر = 'on/upon', کینه = 'hatred', گه = 'place/occasion' used as a locative). پای خویش 'his foot' (پای = 'foot', خویش = 'his own' formed from خوی + clitic ش) is the body-part used in the action; literally 'he pressed down with his foot upon the place of enmity.'
segments.s122-g135: چو is 'when'. دیوان here refers to the supernatural opponents (divs) common in the Shahnameh rather than the listed literary/administrative senses, and it is coordinated with پیلان 'elephants' by و. پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'aggressive/hostile' that modifies both nouns: 'the hostile divs and elephants.'
segments.s122-g136: آورده بودند is a past perfect construction 'they had brought/put' (آورده = 'brought/put', بودند = past auxiliary 'had/ were'); بـــروی ... روی is an idiomatic verbal phrase: بـــروی اندر آوردن/آورده بودندروی = 'to bring one's face forward' = 'to advance / to show one's face' (i.e. to present/turn their faces toward the action). The phrase is somewhat inverted: the verb phrase (آورده بودند) carries the tense while بـــروی ... روی expresses the direction/part ('their faces').
segments.s122-g201: This is a short clause in SOV-adjacent order: the prepositional phrase به لشکر ('to the army') comes first, followed by the temporal/locative adverb گهش (a poetic/archaic form meaning 'at that time' or 'there'), then the indefinite کس ('someone'), and the past verb فرستاد ('he sent'). The final adverb زود ('quickly') reinforces the manner; in poetry manner adverbs often follow the verb for emphasis.
segments.s122-g202: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past used like 'he ordered/said'; تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'so that' or 'that', marking purpose/content. خواسته is a noun meaning 'wish/desire' and هرچ is a correlative 'whatever/whatever it was'; بود is the past copula 'was' completing the subordinate clause ('that whatever was wished'). Word order here places the verb (بود) at the end of the clause, as is normal in Persian subordinate clauses.
segments.s122-g203: This is a poetic list introduced by the preposition ز ('from'). The repeated ز before each major item is a stylistic (and emphatic) poetic device. تخت here means 'throne' (not bed), and در is used as 'door/gate' rather than the locative 'in'. The conjunction و links the items; the sequence is nominal (no verb in this segment).
segments.s122-g204: Continuation of the enumerative list, again beginning with ز ('from'). اسپ is 'steed/horse', سلیح 'weapon/armament', کلاه in this martial context functions as 'helmet' (poetic register uses کلاه for head-gear), and کمر here means 'girdle/belt' (an article of war/equipment). The items are coordinated by و without an overt verb.
segments.s122-g205: نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'to place/set' (they placed). هرجای 'everywhere/wherever' is the locative expression, and چون 'like/as' introduces the simile; the repeated کوه کوه (mountain mountain) is a poetic intensifier meaning 'piled like mountains' or 'mountainous masses'. The verb has already appeared (نهادند) so the simile modifies how/where they placed things.
segments.s122-g206: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/advanced' (a slightly literary form of رفتند). لشکر is the subject 'the army'; همه 'all/the whole' modifies لشکر, and هم‌گروه ('of one group' / 'in the same formation') describes their unity. Word order here is verb (برفتند) + subject phrase, a permitted poetic variation where the verb opens the clause.
segments.s122-g207: This clause states a distributive action: سزاوار ('appropriate/deserving') qualifies هرکس ('each person/whoever'), and ببخشید (here the past sense 'they gave/bestowed') is the verb taking گنج ('treasure') as its object. The adjective/participial quality سزاوار modifies the recipient phrase; Persian often places the verb after the object phrase in such constructions.
segments.s122-g208: به ویژه ('especially') introduces a qualification. کسی کش is a compound where کش is the agentive element ('-kash', 'one who ...'), so کسی‌کش means 'one who takes/seeks' (i.e., the greedy person). فزون ('more/excessive') modifies بود (past copula 'was'), and رنج is the noun 'suffering/hardship' — together: 'especially one who sought more had greater suffering.' This is a common moralizing concluding clause in the epic.
segments.s122-g225: تهمتن is the subject (the hero's name) followed by چنین گفت, a fixed phrase 'thus said' where گفت is the simple past 'said'. با شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the king' marking the addressee; word order (Subject — verb — prepositional complement) is typical of narrative/poetic Persian.
segments.s122-g226: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/which'). هرگونه‌ای is an indefinite phrase 'of any kind/whatever sort', مردم is the subject 'people', and آید is the verbal form (present/subjunctive/optative in literary style) meaning 'come/may come'. به کار is a prepositional phrase 'for the task/work'. The clause therefore gives the content of what was said.
segments.s122-g227: مرا here functions as a topical/possessive/object marker placed before the noun phrase (older/poetic order) — 'to me' / 'of mine'. این هنرها is the noun phrase 'these skills', ز marks origin 'from', اولاد is 'offspring/descendants', and خاست is the past of خاستن 'arose/sprung up'. Overall: 'These skills of mine arose from my offspring.'
segments.s122-g228: که here introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/which'. بر هر سویی is a locative phrase 'on/every side', راه is 'the way', بنمود is the past form 'showed' (بنمود = نمود with the prefix ب- common in classical usage), and راست is an adjective 'straight/right'. The clause modifies the antecedent (here: اولاد/هنرها) and says they 'showed the straight path in every direction.'
segments.s122-g229: به مازندران is a directional/benefactive phrase 'toward Mazandaran' (i.e. hope regarding Mazandaran). دارد is the present third-person auxiliary/lexical verb 'has', اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now', and امید is the noun 'hope'. Word order here places the locative and verb before the noun for emphasis (poetic inversion).
segments.s122-g230: چنین 'thus/so' links this clause to the previous statement. دادمش is a contracted past transitive: دادم 'I gave' +  -ش (third-person indirect object) = 'I gave it to him/them'; راستی را نوید is the object phrase 'the promise/news of truth' where راستی is 'truth', را is the definite/object marker, and نوید here means 'promise' (sense 'promise/omen'). Overall: 'I thus gave him the truthful promise.'
segments.s122-g231: کنون 'now' sets the time. خلعت شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's robe (of honour)'. باید is the modal 'must/ought to' that governs what follows, and نخست is the adverb 'first'. The phrase states an obligation regarding the royal robe (typical classical word order: time — noun phrase — modal — adverb).
segments.s122-g232: یکی 'one/a single' modifies the coordinated object 'عهد و مهری' (an oath/covenant and a 'mehrī' — here best read as a token/seal/dowry-related item). و is 'and'. بروبر is an adverbial/compound meaning 'face-to-face' or 'front to front', and درست is an adjective/adverb 'proper/accurate'. The line prescribes that, first, there should be one covenant and a seal presented properly and in person.
segments.s122-g145: ازان پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'after that' (ازان = thereupon/then + پس = after). تهمتن is the subject (a proper epithet/name). یکی modifies نیزه and here means 'one' or 'a single'. نیزه is the direct object 'spear'. خواست is the past tense of خواستن ('he wanted/asked for'); the verb follows the object in this poetic word order.
segments.s122-g146: سوی + noun is the directional prepositional phrase 'toward(s)'. شاه مازندران is the noun phrase 'the king of Mazandaran' (Mazandaran here as the legendary realm). تاخت is a past verb 'he rushed/charged' and راست is an adverb 'straight/directly' modifying تاخت; the verb-final order is typical in the epic register.
segments.s122-g147: چو at the clause head means 'when'. بر نیزه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the spear' placed before the subject; رستم is the subject proper name. افگند is the past tense 'threw/cast' (poetic form of انداخت). چشم ('eye') is the object of افگند in the idiom 'چشم افگندن' = 'to cast a glance'.
segments.s122-g148: نماند is the past negative sense 'did not remain / was left'. ایچ (archaic) = 'nothing/none' — here a negated absolutive 'not a thing remained'. با او = 'with him'. دلیری و خشم are the two nouns 'bravery and anger' which are said not to remain; the conjunction و links them. Word order places the negative predicate before the nouns for emphasis.
segments.s122-g149: یکی here is an indefinite 'one / someone (one of them)'. نیزه is the instrument and زد is the past of زدن 'struck/thrust'. بر + کمربند 'on/at the belt' marks the target. اوی is an archaic/emphatic third‑person pronoun referring back to the owner of the belt ('his'). Word order (verb after object phrase) reflects poetic arrangement.
segments.s122-g150: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (source/agent). گبر means 'gabr' (an unbeliever/infidel) — used here as the opposing fighter. اندر آمد is a compound past 'entered in / went into' (اندر = in, آمد = came). به پیوند اوی literally 'into his پیوند' — پیوند in epic usage can mean a joining/part of the body (here 'flesh/side'), and اوی is the possessive pronoun 'his'. The whole clause says the spear went in from the enemy into his body.
segments.s122-g151: شد is the past 'became' (intransitive change of state). از جادویی sets the cause/agent 'by/through magic' (جادویی = magical/sorcerous). تن + ش is the common poetic possessive construction 'تنش' = 'his body' (تن = body, ش = his). لخت کوه is a nominal phrase 'a bare/naked mountain' (لخت = bare; کوه = mountain). The clause says his body by magic turned into a single bare mountain.
segments.s122-g152: از ایران is a source phrase 'from Iran' (here meaning the Iranian side/realm). بروبر is an adverb 'face-to-face / directly opposite' modifying نظاره (sight/view). نظاره گروه = 'the view (or gaze) of the group/troop' — together the phrase means that this mountain-like body confronted the sight of the Iranian host. Word order places the source first, then manner, then the affected perceivers.
segments.s122-g209: ز is the poetic/pre-classical form of از (‘from’); it begins a prepositional phrase. دیوان here means the court or men of the court (not a poetry-collection); it is the noun governed by ز. هرآنکس که introduces a free relative clause 'whoever ...', with که as the relative marker. بد ناسپاس is a two-word adjectival description modifying هرآنکس ('bad/unfavorable and ungrateful'), the adjective بد before the more specific ناسپاس (ungrateful) for emphasis.
segments.s122-g210: وز here functions as a poetic connective roughly equivalent to 'and/from' (archaic usage). ایشان is the third-person plural demonstrative/pronoun 'them' (referring back to the court men). دل انجمن is a genitival phrase 'the heart of the assembly' (دل governed by انجمن). پرهراس is a predicative adjective 'full of dread/fear', so the clause reads 'and from them the assembly's heart was fearful.'
segments.s122-g211: بفرمودشان is the compound verb form (بـ + فرمود + شان) meaning 'he commanded them' (فرمود with the accusative/clitic شان). تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that') and governs the following clause. بریدند is 3rd person plural past tense 'they cut (off)'; سر is the object 'head' and follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s122-g212: فگندند is an archaic perfect/past plural 'they threw' (ف- prefix is an older allomorph). جایی is an adverbial 'somewhere' or 'a place'. که introduces a relative clause 'where/that', here functioning as 'where'. بد modifies رهگذر, giving 'an evil/bad/unlucky passerby'; رهگذر is the noun 'passerby, wayfarer'. The clause thus describes where they cast the heads.
segments.s122-g213: وز here is an archaic/poetic connective introducing the sequence; آن پس together mean 'after that' or 'then'. بیامد is simple past 'he came'. به جای نماز literally 'to the place of prayer' (به + جای = 'to the place/position of'); جای can also mean 'in the stead of' but here is spatial 'place'. نماز is the noun 'prayer', the destination of بیامد.
segments.s122-g214: همی is an adverb marking iterative or continuous action (often 'ever/continually' in older Persian). گفت here is used as the past verb 'he spoke/said' (the supplied sense list treated گفت oddly as a noun, but functionally it is a past-tense verb). با introduces the companion/agent of the speech: 'with'. داور پاک is a compound nominal phrase 'the Pure/Judge' (داور = judge; پاک = pure), and راز functions adverbially or as the content marker 'in secret' (با ... راز = 'with secret / in secret with ...'). The whole segment means 'he spoke in secret with the pure Judge.'
segments.s122-g215: به یک هفته is a temporal expression 'for one week' or 'during a week' (به + numeric duration). بر پیش is an older phrasing meaning 'in the presence of' or 'before' (بر + پیش + [object]). یزدان پاک is the divine epithet 'the Pure God' (یزدان = God/the divine; پاک = pure). Together the phrase sets the time and the locus: 'for a week before the Pure God.'
segments.s122-g216: همی again signals a continuous or ongoing action. با نیایش 'with supplication/prayer' is an adverbial phrase describing manner. بپیمود is the past/punctual form of پیمودن (بـ prefix + پیمود), meaning 'he traversed/covered/accomplished' — in spiritual contexts it can mean to perform ritual prostrations or to traverse the earth in devotion. خاک is the object 'earth/ground' or figuratively 'dust', so the clause means 'he, with prayer, traversed/covered the ground (i.e. performed humble prostrations/prayer).'
segments.s122-g153: تهمتن (proper name) is the sentence subject. فرو ماند is a compound verbal phrase: فرو (a prefixed adverb/adverbial element 'down/into') + ماند (past tense 'remained/was left'), here meaning 'was left/stood in a state of'. اندر شگفت is a locative/prepositional phrase placed after the verb ('in/within wonder'), so the clause literally reads 'Tahmatan was left into (a) wonder.'
segments.s122-g154: This is a simple SOV clause: سناندار (the spear‑bearer, subject) نیزه (direct object) به گردن (prepositional phrase 'to/at the neck') گرفت (past tense 'took/seized'). The idiom به گردن گرفتن here conveys taking/placing or gripping something at/near the neck; گرفت is the past simple third‑person form.
segments.s122-g155: رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached' placed before the locative phrase (classical narrative order). اندر آن جای is the locative 'in/at that place', and کاووس شاه is the subject in apposition (name + title), so the literal order is 'arrived in that place Kavus the king.'
segments.s122-g156: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing the equipments/forces accompanying him. ابا here is an archaic/poetic variant of با ('with') introducing the list; then پیل, کوس, درفش و سپاه are the linked nouns ('elephant(s), war‑drum(s), banner, and army'). The conjunction و connects the items in the usual way.
segments.s122-g157: به رستم is the indirect object 'to Rostam', followed by چنين گفت ('thus said')—an idiomatic inversion where the verb 'said' appears after the adverb. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and سرفراز is the vocative epithet 'proud/victorious one'; the whole chunk is the speech introducer 'he said to Rostam thus: "O, proud one..."'.
segments.s122-g158: This is a colloquial/classical question clause introduced by چه بودت که — literally 'what was it that...' (چه = what; بودت = 'was to you' / 'affected you'), followed by ایدر بماندی دراز ('you remained here long'): ایدر = here, بماندی = second‑person past/subjunctive form 'you stayed/remained', دراز = 'long'. The construction asks for the reason something caused the prolonged stay.
segments.s122-g159: بدو ('to him') marks the indirect object; گفت رستم is the reporting clause 'Rostam said'. که introduces the subordinate clause/explanation. چون رزم سخت is a temporal/conditional phrase 'when/as the battle is fierce' (چون = when/as; رزم = battle; سخت = hard/terrible).
segments.s122-g160: ببود is an archaic past tense copular/auxiliary 'was' used in narrative (classical form). The clause continues with و (and) + بیفروخت (literally 'did not sell' or idiomatically 'did not let go/refused to part with') modifying پیروز بخت ('victorious fortune'). Together the meaning is 'and victorious fortune did not abandon/refuse to part (with the hero)'.
segments.s122-g113: گرازه (a feminine pig) is the subject, followed by همی شد, an older/poetic periphrastic past that gives a durative/emphatic past sense (‘became/was turning’). بسان is a simile marker 'like', and گراز is the noun being compared to. Word order is Subject – (adverbial/modal) – predicate – simile phrase.
segments.s122-g114: درفشی is the banner/standard or its bearer; برافراخته is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'raised/upright' describing the banner; هفت یاز is a numeral phrase where یاز here functions adverbially ('again/repeatedly') modifying the raising—i.e. the raising happened repeatedly/seven times. The phrase modifies the prior clause (how the banner was shown).
segments.s122-g115: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate temporal clause. The list of names is joined with و ('and'), so the whole noun phrase is the subject of the coming verb. Note that classical Persian often strings names with و without other markers.
segments.s122-g116: برفتند is a past‑tense 3rd‑person plural of رفتن 'they went/advanced'; با introduces accompaniment 'with', and نامداران is a plural noun meaning 'renowned/illustrious (men)' that functions as the complement of با. نیو (ney) follows as an accompanying instrument or attribute—here 'with famed men and a ney'.
segments.s122-g117: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet as subject. به + noun + اندر آمد is an idiomatic classical sequence meaning 'entered into' or 'came into' (به … اندر آمد = 'came into …'); نخست ('first') is an adverb modifying the whole action, placed after the verb phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s122-g118: زمین را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker; به here marks the instrumental/means ('by/with')—به خون دلیران بشست = 'washed (it) with the blood of the brave'. بشست is a past transitive verb 'he washed', so the clause literally: 'He washed the earth with the blood of the brave.'
segments.s122-g119: چو again introduces a temporal 'when' clause. گودرز is the agent/subject; کشواد (a personal name here) follows and is the object/role placed 'بر میمنه'—بر + میمنه = 'on/upon the right (side)'. Note the postposition بر before the location noun (meimneh) which is typical in older Persian.
segments.s122-g120: This is a nominal series of objects/what was taken or borne: سلیح 'weapons' and سپه 'the host/army' followed by برد (here functioning in the poem as the verb 'carried/took' in context). The list continues with کوس 'war‑drum' and بنه 'bundle/baggage'. In poetry such coordination can compactly list accoutrements and troops.
segments.s122-g233: که تا زنده باشد به مازندران is a subordinate temporal clause: که introduces the clause ('that/as'), تا introduces the duration ('until/as long as'), and زنده باشد is a present-subjunctive/optative form meaning 'be/remain alive' (classical narrative uses باشد for a dependent-state). به + مازندران gives the location/direction ('in/to Mazandaran') following the verb phrase.
segments.s122-g234: پرستش کنندش همه مهتران is the main clause: پرستش is a noun 'worship' used with the light verb کنند (کنند‑ش = 'they do it / they worship him') where the clitic -ش marks the object 'him'. همه مهتران (literally 'all mehterān') is the plural subject/agent following the verb in this poetic order — 'all the chiefs/elders will worship him.'
segments.s122-g235: چو بشنید گفتار خسرو پرست is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', بشنید is past simple 'he heard', and گفتار خسرو پرست is a noun phrase 'the speech/words of the Khosrow‑worshipper' (خسرو as name, پرست as a follower/worshipper element modifying گفتار).
segments.s122-g236: به بر زد جهاندار بیدار دست is a compact poetic clause where the preverb بر + verb زد = 'struck upon/struck', with به marking direction/target; جهاندار بیدار is a noun modified by the adjective بیدار ('the alert/awake sovereign'), and دست (hand) is the object placed at the end: 'the awake sovereign struck (his) hand upon (his chest).' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s122-g237: سپرد آن زمان تخت شاهی بدوی begins with the verb سپرد (past 'he entrusted/handed over'). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time' (آن modifying زمان), and تخت شاهی بدوی is a noun phrase 'the original/primordial royal throne' where descriptive words (شاهی, بدوی) follow the noun in classical order.
segments.s122-g238: وزانجا سوی پارس بنهاد روی is built around the directional phrase وزانجا ('and from there') + سوی پارس ('toward Pars'), followed by the verb بنهاد (classical past 'placed/set') and روی ('face'). In context this functions idiomatically as 'he set/turned his face toward Pars'; the prepositional phrase precedes the verb for rhythmic/poetic order.
segments.s122-g137: جهانجوی (compound noun/epithet) is the subject phrase 'world-seeker' (an epithet for a hero); کرد is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'did/made/acted' used here as the past narrative verb 'remembered/caused to remember'. از + جهاندار یاد is a prepositional-object phrase: از here marks the person 'of/about' (i.e., 'of the sovereign') and یاد is the noun 'remembrance' — together 'کرد از جهاندار یاد' = 'he remembered the sovereign' (word order: subject + past verb + prepositional object; poetic compression omits an explicit direct object marker).
segments.s122-g138: سنان‌دار نیزه is a descriptive noun phrase 'the spear (that is) point‑bearing/pointed'; به + دارنده داد is a ditransitive-like construction where به marks the indirect object 'to/for the holder/bearer' and داد is the past 3rd‑person singular 'gave/handed'. English idiom requires turning the adjective‑like سنان‌دار into 'pointed spear' and making دارنده the recipient 'its bearer'.
segments.s122-g139: برآهیخت is a past tense (3rd sg.) verbal compound meaning 'rose up/was roused' or 'lifted up' — here it takes گرز ('mace/club') as its object: 'he lifted the mace'. The coordinating و links the next verbal phrase: برآورد جوش — برآورد (past) 'brought forth/caused' and جوش is an abstract noun 'surge/boiling/impetuous rush' so together 'and he brought forth a surge' (i.e., aroused a tumult).
segments.s122-g140: هوا گشت is 'the air/atmosphere became' (گشت = become/turned). از marks source/cause ('from/by'), and آواز او 'his voice/cry' is the cause. پرخروش is a predicate adjective 'boisterous/turbulent' describing هوا. Word order is typical Persian: subject (هوا) + verb (گشت) + prepositional phrase giving cause + adjective predicate.
segments.s122-g141: برآورد here again is a past 3rd‑person verb 'raised/brought forth' or 'caused to appear'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. گرد is an archaic/ambiguous element (poetic; here part of a compound epithet) and immediately followed by سالار کش, where سالار is 'commander/chief' and کش is the agentive element '‑killer' (forming 'slayer of commanders' or 'chief‑slayer'). Grammatically this is a nominal phrase dependent on the verb 'برآورد' — 'he raised/brought forth that ...'.
segments.s122-g142: نه ... و نه is the paired negation 'neither ... nor ...'. هر 'با' introduces the means/agent 'with/by': نه با دیو جان = 'not with (a) demon-life' (literally 'not by the demon's life/strength') and نه با پیل هش = 'nor with the elephant — alas' (هش is an interjection/exclamation). In short, the clause denies that the opponent was overcome by demon or by elephant; the negative construction scopes over both alternatives.
segments.s122-g143: فگنده is a past participial/adjectival form 'thrown/scattered' describing the state of the plain. همه دشت is 'the whole plain' (همه + noun = 'all/entire'), and خرطوم پیل is a noun phrase 'elephant trunks' (خرطوم = trunk, پیل = elephant). The sequence means 'the whole plain was strewn with elephant trunks' — adjective + noun phrase describes scene.
segments.s122-g144: همه کشته دیدند is a simple past clause 'they saw all (of them) killed' or 'they saw all the slain' (همه as quantifier, کشته 'slain' as noun/adjective, دیدند past 'they saw'). بر چند میل is a prepositional/time/extent phrase 'for/over several miles' (بر + extent); چند here means 'several/a few'. میل in this context is being used as a measure ('miles') — the specific sense of میل is not listed among provided options, so it is marked with -1.
segments.s122-g161: This clause is a slightly inverted, poetic order of a simple past action: مَرا (me + object marker را split here) + دید (saw) + چون شاه مازندران (when/as the King of Mazandaran). چُون here introduces the agent/time ('when' or 'as') and the full subject phrase follows the verb for metrical effect; the object مَرا appears before the verb, marked by را.
segments.s122-g162: This participial/adverbial phrase describes the king’s posture or action: به + گردن (on/over the neck) + برآورده (literally 'raised' or 'having lifted') + گرز گران (a heavy mace). The preposition به introduces the locus/position ('upon/over his neck'); گرز گران is a noun + adjective ('heavy mace') modifying the action.
segments.s122-g163: This clause is transitive: به رخش دلاور ('to/onto his/ my steed, the brave one') + سپردم (I entrusted/gave over) + عنان (the reins). The verb سپردم is past tense, first person singular; the prepositional phrase (به رخش دلاور) indicates the recipient/target of the entrusting.
segments.s122-g164: This is a second coordinated past action with an object expressed by a prepositional phrase: زدم (I struck) + بر (against/on) + کمربند (the belt) + گبرش (of the gabr/infidel) + سنان (with a spear). The instrument ('spear') is placed immediately after the affected noun phrase for compact poetic effect.
segments.s122-g165: گمانم (my thought / my suspicion) + چنان بد (so bad/ill) + که (that) introduces a result clause: او شد نگون (he became overturned/ruined). شد is past tense 'became' and نگون is a predicative adjective meaning 'overthrown/ill‑fated'; the whole unit expresses a fearful supposition that resulted in ruin.
segments.s122-g166: A short temporal/motion clause: کنون (now) + آید (he comes) + از کوهه (out of the mountain) + زین برون (with saddle/out of the saddle). آید is present/future‑oriented in form but here reports immediate motion; the locative از + کوهه marks the source and برون (‘out’) emphasizes emergence.
segments.s122-g167: بر این‌گونه شد ('in/according to this manner it happened') + سنگ در پیش من ('a stone/rock was before me'). The impersonal/poetic بر این‌گونه شد frames what follows as the resulting condition; در پیش من is a fixed locative phrase meaning 'in front of me' or 'before me.'
segments.s122-g168: This negative clause states lack of knowledge: نبود آگه ('there was no awareness' / 'I was not aware') + از رای کم بیش من (of the counsel/plan, whether lesser or greater, of mine/his — here meaning 'of his counsel' or 'of the plan' depending on context). نبود is the past negative of بودن; رای کم بیش is a compact pair meaning 'little or much' (= in any degree).
