segments.s122-g217: بهشتم is the fused form of بهشت + ـم (my paradise); it functions as the patient/theme. در گنجها is a locative phrase 'in/among the treasures.' کرد is the past tense verb 'made/did' and with باز after it yields a causative/ restitutive sense: 'made/returned ... again' (i.e. 'restored'). The order (possessed noun + locative, then verb + باز) is idiomatic poetic Persian.
segments.s122-g218: ببخشید is a past-tense verb meaning 'bestowed/gave' here (not 'forgave'). بر هرکه is a prepositional phrase 'upon whoever.' بودش is an enclitic/archaic past form meaning 'was / he had' attached to the following predicate, and نیاز is the nominal predicate 'need' — together 'whoever had need.'
segments.s122-g219: همی before a verb is an archaic/progressive marker meaning 'kept on / continued.' گشت is the past verb 'went/turned/continued.' یک هفته is a duration expression ('for a week') following the verb, and زین گونه نیز is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner also' (زین is an archaic form meaning 'this').
segments.s122-g220: ببخشید (past) 'he bestowed/gave.' آن را is the definite object with را marking 'that (thing).' The relative/qualifier clause که بایست چیز follows: که introduces 'which/that,' بایست is a modal/obligative 'ought/was necessary,' and چیز is 'thing' — together 'that thing which ought to be (given).' Note the verb-first poetic ordering places the giving before the explicit object phrase.
segments.s122-g221: سیم هفته (here rendered as 'in the third week') is the temporal frame; چون functions as a subordinating conjunction 'when/as.' کارها is the subject 'the affairs/matters,' گشت is the past verb 'became/turned,' and راست is the predicate/adjective 'right/straight' — 'when the affairs became right.' Word order places the time phrase first and the subordinate clause after it.
segments.s122-g222: می‌و is an archaic/poetic noun related to juice/wine (here functioning as 'wine' or 'drink'), جام یاقوت is a compound noun phrase 'a cup (or goblet) of ruby' i.e. a ruby cup or gem‑studded goblet, و links items, میخواره is a noun 'wine‑drinker' (or possibly a cup‑bearer/drunkard), and خواست is the past-tense verb 'he wanted.' The listed goods/companions precede the verb, which closes the clause.
segments.s122-g223: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase 'after a week / one week later' (به used idiomatically for time). با ویژگان means 'with the distinguished/nobles' (ویژگان here = 'the distinguished ones'). می‌به is an archaic poetic pairing (here functioning to introduce 'wine' as part of a list) and چنگ is 'harp' — together a terse enumerative phrase 'with wine and harp.' The whole segment lists time, companions, and accompanying items before the main verb.
segments.s122-g224: به مازندران کرد is a motion construction: به + place + کرد ('he made [toward] Mazandaran' = he went to Mazandaran). زان پس is an adverbial 'after that / thereafter' (زان = 'from that'), and درنگ is a noun 'pause/delay'; together (with کرد) the clause states that thereafter he made a pause. The verb کرد is the past action anchoring the motion and subsequent action.
segments.s122-g185: 'بدو گفت' is a light prepositional + verb sequence: بدو (be + third-person clitic) = 'to him/unto him', here marking the indirect object ('he said to him'); گفت would normally be the past 'said' (the supplied lemma senses don't list the verb meaning). ار is the conditional 'if'; ایدونک is a colloquial/poetic particle meaning 'thus/this way'; پیداشوی is پید ا + شوی: پیدا 'visible/appear' plus شوی (2nd-singular present/subjunctive of شدن) = 'you become/you show yourself' (a conditional/subjunctive sense: 'if you appear').
segments.s122-g186: به + (noun) gives a circumstantial phrase meaning 'in/into the form of' here; گردی is the noun 'shape/form/guise' in this context (the supplied senses don't list 'form' exactly, so the grammatical role is 'in the guise of X'); ازین = از + این 'of/from this'; تنبل و جادوی are two modifiers: تنبل 'lazy' and جادوی 'magical' ( adjectival/attributive ), describing the person/guise referred to.
segments.s122-g187: وگرنه = 'otherwise' (introductory adversative). The repeated به before each weapon is the instrumental/agentive preposition ('by/with'); گرز, تیغ, تبر are listed as instruments (mace/club, sword, axe). The coordination 'و ... و ... و' strings the weapons together as the means by which the action will be done.
segments.s122-g188: ببرم is a 1st-person present/future-like form (subjunctive/optative or present-future 'I will/shall...') of برد/بر in the supplied list but here used intransitively as 'I will cut/hew' (the supplied senses don't list that exact nuance). همه سنگ را = direct object 'all the stones' (را marks the definite object). سر به سر is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'one by one' / 'one after another' or 'to pieces' depending on context; word order places the verb before its object (typical in classical Persian).
segments.s122-g189: چو here = 'when' (temporal conjunction). بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'; شد is the copula/verb 'became' (so 'when he heard it, he became ...'). چون یکی پاره ابر is a simile 'like a single/torn piece of cloud' (چون = 'like/as', یکی = 'one/a', پاره ابر = 'a torn piece of cloud').
segments.s122-g190: This clause describes his sudden martial appearance. به + سر + (برش) reads as an inverted, somewhat compressed word order meaning 'on his head ...'; پولاد = 'steel' (i.e. a helmet or steel cap) is the material placed on the head. و بر تنش = 'and on his body' (بر = 'on/upon', تن = 'body', ش = possessive clitic 'his'). گبر here names the kind of body-armour or an epithet applied to his apparel in the poem; the supplied lemma/senses for برش and گبر do not exactly match the usage, so their precise lexical senses are irregular/poetic.
segments.s122-g191: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet (Tahmātan); گرفت is the simple past 'seized/grasped'. آن زمان 'that time' is a temporal adverbial ('at that moment'). دست اوی = 'his hand' where دست is the noun and اوی is the enclitic/old-form possessive 'his'. The phrase is straightforward S (subject) V (گرفت) O (دست اوی) with a temporal adjunct.
segments.s122-g192: بخندید = 'he laughed' (past). و = 'and' links two actions. زی here is an archaic/poetic prepositional particle meaning 'toward/before' (the supplied senses are limited; it functions as a directional/prepositional marker), شاه = 'the king', بنهاد روی = بنهاد (put/placed/turned) + روی 'face' = 'he turned/placed his face' i.e. 'he turned his face toward the king' — an archaic phrasing for 'he faced the king'.
segments.s122-g169: برین گونه is an archaic/poetic locative phrase meaning 'in this manner' (بر + این). خارا is the subject noun 'the flint/rock'. یکی کوه گشت is a predicative phrase: یکی ('one' / indefinite) + کُوه ('mountain') + گشت (past of گَشتن 'to become/turn'), so literally 'became a mountain.' Note the verb گشت (perfective past) links the subject خارا to the resulting predicate.
segments.s122-g170: ز جنگ و ز مردی is a pair of causal/ablative prepositional phrases ('through/from battle and through/from manliness/valor'), with repeated ز for emphasis. بی‌اندوه is a predicate adjective 'without sorrow / free of grief.' گشت (again past of گَشتن) predicates the state: 'it became without sorrow.'
segments.s122-g171: به لشکر is a locative/directional phrase 'to/into the army/host.' گهش is an archaic adverbial/nominal element (گه + -ش) meaning 'to its place/occasion' or 'to the camp/time of the host.' برد here is used as 'bringing/bringing victory' (classical texts often use بردن in this sense); باید + کنون is the modal construction 'it must now / now it is necessary.' Note classical word order allows the verbal noun/verb before باید: برد باید کنون = 'now must (one) bring/take.'
segments.s122-g172: مگر is the conditional/exclusion 'unless.' کاید is an older/poetic present-tense verb meaning 'he/one devises, plots, contrives.' از ... برون is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from ... out' with سنگ خارا 'the flinty stone' as its complement: 'to get out of the flinty stone.' برون is the adverb 'out/forth.'
segments.s122-g173: ز لشکر 'from the host/army' is the source phrase. هر آن کس که introduces a relative clause 'every one who...'. بد زورمند is an archaic adjectival phrase: زورمند 'mighty' is modified by بد, here functioning as an emphatic/older intensifier (classical usage) — so 'very mighty' or 'mighty indeed.' The clause describes which people are meant.
segments.s122-g174: بسودند is a past-tense verb meaning 'they used/benefited (from/put to advantage).' چَنگ is here 'claw/talon' (not the musical instrument) and آزمودند is the past 'they tested/tried.' بند is 'fetter/bond' (they bound/fettered). Together the clause says they employed the mighty for gain, tested the claw, and bound (it/him) — verbs in past tense describe completed actions.
segments.s122-g175: نه is the negation 'not.' برخاست is the past tense 'rose/moved/was removed.' از جای is the prepositional phrase 'from its place.' سنگ گران is the noun phrase 'the heavy/weighty stone' (subject/agent in sense: 'the stone did not move'). The negative precedes the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s122-g176: میان اندرون functions as a locative nominal group 'in the middle/within the inner part' (میان = middle, اندرون = inside). شاه مازندران is a genitive construction 'the King of Mazandaran' (possessor follows the possessed). The whole segment gives the location: 'in the inner chamber of the King of Mazandaran.'
